items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!".

rules_checked.reasoning: The item is declared as kind 'type' and it genuinely is a new public type (Greeter) with two methods. It is not a protocol, not a function-only module, not a rename/removal — 'type' is the correct kind for this unit of work.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift lives inside the Greeter target, which Package.swift defines as a library target with a matching GreeterTests test target depending on it. This is the only target in the package and is the natural home for the Greeter type; the module is a proper fit.

This is a standalone library module with no view layer or view-model layer present in this package — there is no UI/service layering in this walking-skeleton package, so no domain logic is being placed in a view or data access performed from a view model. Not applicable / no violation.

Sources/Greeter currently only contains GreeterModule.swift, an empty namespace-marker enum with no behavior. Adding the actual Greeter type with greet(name:) and greet() logic in its own file is warranted — it's the first real logic-bearing type for this feature and doesn't duplicate or awkwardly extend the empty marker enum. SRP is respected: one type owns the greeting behavior.

`Greeter` is a noun naming a concrete responsibility-bearing type; `greet(name:)` and `greet()` are verb-named methods with a clear argument label, matching Swift API Design Guidelines (methods that perform actions read as verb phrases, argument labels needed for clarity are present).
No protocol is introduced by this item — it's a concrete type only. No speculative abstraction is being added, so this rule is satisfied trivially.

The item is a self-contained type with no described imports beyond the standard library; it does not cross into other modules or smuggle a forbidden import. Dependency direction (Tests -> Greeter) is preserved and unaffected by this item.
